(also **Goblin Legacy**, **Back to the Goblin**, **New Goblin**, **Goblin Rebirth**, the **Goblin Keys**, **the Goblins** and **[Claudio Simonetti](/wiki/Claudio_Simonetti "Claudio Simonetti")'s Goblin**) is an Italian [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") band known for their [film scores](/wiki/Film_score "Film score"). They frequently collaborate with [Dario Argento](/wiki/Dario_Argento "Dario Argento"), most notably creating the scores for *[Profondo Rosso](/wiki/Profondo_Rosso_(soundtrack) "Profondo Rosso (soundtrack)")* in 1975 and *[Suspiria](/wiki/Suspiria_(1977_soundtrack) "Suspiria (1977 soundtrack)")* in 1977. Because their collaborator Dario Argento specializes in creating horror, suspense and slasher/giallo genre movies, scores made by Goblin in these movies often had eerie and ominous tones. CD re-releases of their scores have performed well, especially in Germany and Japan. Goblin returned with a series of live concerts in Europe in 2009 and in North America in 2013.

(they had done some live shows as Oliver), their early work spawned one [eponymous](/wiki/Eponymous "Eponymous") progressive rock record, and they were then called in to compose the score for *Profondo Rosso*. The band changed their name to Goblin, rewriting most of the score, originally written by [Giorgio Gaslini](/wiki/Giorgio_Gaslini "Giorgio Gaslini") including the famous main [theme](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music"). The 1975 soundtrack album was a huge hit.[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*] After a reshuffle in their line-up, they put out an instrumental progressive rock album *[Roller](/wiki/Roller_(Goblin_album) "Roller (Goblin album)")*, before working with Argento again for 1977's *Suspiria*. Some tracks from the album *Roller* were used in the Italian version of [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero")'s film *[Martin](/wiki/Martin_(1977_film) "Martin (1977 film)")* replacing those from the original soundtrack written by [Donald Rubinstein](/wiki/Donald_Rubinstein "Donald Rubinstein"). This was because at that time Goblin's music had become very popular in Italy following the successes of *Profondo rosso*. Other film scores and a concept album (*Il Fantastico Viaggio Del Bagarozzo Mark*) followed, then the score for the European version of [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero")'s 1978 *[Dawn of the Dead](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Dead_(1978_film) "Dawn of the Dead (1978 film)")*. In both this and *Suspiria*'s opening title sequences, they are credited as "The Goblins with Dario Argento". Tracks 1, 2 and 7 from the European version are also in the American version of the film.

**Goblin** (also **Goblin Legacy**, **Back to the Goblin**, **New Goblin**, **Goblin Rebirth**, the **Goblin Keys**, **the Goblins** and **[Claudio Simonetti](/wiki/Claudio_Simonetti "Claudio Simonetti")'s Goblin**) is an Italian [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") band known for their [film scores](/wiki/Film_score "Film score"). They frequently collaborate with [Dario Argento](/wiki/Dario_Argento "Dario Argento"), most notably creating the scores for *[Profondo Rosso](/wiki/Profondo_Rosso_(soundtrack) "Profondo Rosso (soundtrack)")* in 1975 and *[Suspiria](/wiki/Suspiria_(1977_soundtrack) "Suspiria (1977 soundtrack)")* in 1977. Because their collaborator Dario Argento specializes in creating horror, suspense and slasher/giallo genre movies, scores made by Goblin in these movies often had eerie and ominous tones. CD re-releases of their scores have performed well, especially in Germany and Japan. Goblin returned with a series of live concerts in Europe in 2009 and in North America in 2013.

Initially recording as **Cherry Five** (they had done some live shows as Oliver), their early work spawned one [eponymous](/wiki/Eponymous "Eponymous") progressive rock record, and they were then called in to compose the score for *Profondo Rosso*. The band changed their name to Goblin, rewriting most of the score, originally written by [Giorgio Gaslini](/wiki/Giorgio_Gaslini "Giorgio Gaslini") including the famous main [theme](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music"). The 1975 soundtrack album was a huge hit.[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*] After a reshuffle in their line-up, they put out an instrumental progressive rock album *[Roller](/wiki/Roller_(Goblin_album) "Roller (Goblin album)")*, before working with Argento again for 1977's *Suspiria*. Some tracks from the album *Roller* were used in the Italian version of [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero")'s film *[Martin](/wiki/Martin_(1977_film) "Martin (1977 film)")* replacing those from the original soundtrack written by [Donald Rubinstein](/wiki/Donald_Rubinstein "Donald Rubinstein"). This was because at that time Goblin's music had become very popular in Italy following the successes of *Profondo rosso*. Other film scores and a concept album (*Il Fantastico Viaggio Del Bagarozzo Mark*) followed, then the score for the European version of [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero")'s 1978 *[Dawn of the Dead](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Dead_(1978_film) "Dawn of the Dead (1978 film)")*. In both this and *Suspiria*'s opening title sequences, they are credited as "The Goblins with Dario Argento". Tracks 1, 2 and 7 from the European version are also in the American version of the film.

Despite their success, membership continued to be a revolving door. The remaining members continued to work on further scores, and there was a partial reunification of three of the four band members for Argento's *[Tenebrae](/wiki/Tenebrae_(soundtrack) "Tenebrae (soundtrack)")* (1982), credited to the three band members, but not as Goblin. The last collaboration with Argento took place in 2001, with the film *[Sleepless](/wiki/Sleepless_(2001_film) "Sleepless (2001 film)")*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Oliver, 1972–1975

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Oliver, 1972–1975")]

Between 1972 and 1973, [Claudio Simonetti](/wiki/Claudio_Simonetti "Claudio Simonetti") (keyboards) and [Massimo Morante](/wiki/Massimo_Morante "Massimo Morante") (guitars), aided by [Fabio Pignatelli](/wiki/Fabio_Pignatelli "Fabio Pignatelli") (bass guitar) and Walter Martino (drums), recorded some demo tapes using the name Oliver.

On a trip to London, while looking for contacts, the band bumped into [Eddy Offord](/wiki/Eddy_Offord "Eddy Offord"), then producer of [Yes](/wiki/Yes_(band) "Yes (band)"). After listening to a demo tape, the tycoon producer expressed interest and asked them to move to England. By then, Fabio Pignatelli had become a steady member, and the band found a regular drummer, [Carlo Bordini](/wiki/Carlo_Bordini "Carlo Bordini"), and an English lead vocalist ([Clive Haynes](/w/index.php?title=Clive_Haynes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clive Haynes (page does not exist)")). After many months of rehearsing, they returned to London while Offord was on tour with Yes in the US; after many performances and various attempts at striking deals with record houses, they were forced to go back to Italy, due to lack of funds.

### Cherry Five

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cherry Five")]

Back home, they signed with [Cinevox](/wiki/Cinevox "Cinevox"), and Clive Haynes was replaced by [Tony Tartarini](/w/index.php?title=Tony_Tartarini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tony Tartarini (page does not exist)") who had previously recorded with L'Uovo Di Colombo under the name Toni Gionta. The band's name was changed to Cherry Five by the label, and according to Claudio Simonetti for no apparent reason, as the members themselves had intended to continue as "Oliver." Whatever debate about the band's name there may have existed, their first LP was titled *Cherry Five*. Cinevox Records was active mainly in soundtrack publishing. Due to the band's peculiar sound, the band was frequently called to perform and arrange famous musicians' compositions. This helped them better understand the world of soundtracks and the particular techniques required. Their final act as Cherry Five was to drop new drummer Carlo Bordini and bring back Walter Martino on drums.

### Success and film scores, 1975–1978

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Success and film scores, 1975–1978")]

At the beginning of 1975, the band began a cooperation with Giorgio Gaslini on the *[Profondo rosso](/wiki/Profondo_rosso "Profondo rosso")* film project. The band replaced Martino (who went on to join the band Libra) with [Agostino Marangolo](/w/index.php?title=Agostino_Marangolo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Agostino Marangolo (page does not exist)") (of the band Flea on the Honey/Flea/Etna) during this period. Martino recorded on all but two cuts of *Profondo rosso*.

By chance, after three or four days of recording, Gaslini left the film after a conflict with [Dario Argento](/wiki/Dario_Argento "Dario Argento"), so Argento decided to try the band's hand at composing, giving them one night to write a score, and one day to record it. To distinguish this new release from their first LP which was just about to be marketed, the band changed their name again, this time to **Goblin**. Their success exceeded all expectations: more than one million copies sold, enjoying 52 weeks on the Italian hit charts and ranking first in both the singles and LP categories. It launched a highly successful period for the band, which came to an end in 1978 when the band split after the recording of Dario Argento's cut of George Romero's *[Zombi](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Dead_(soundtracks)#Zombi_by_Goblin "Dawn of the Dead (soundtracks)")* (also known as *[Dawn of the Dead](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Dead_(1978_film) "Dawn of the Dead (1978 film)")*). (On the score for the European version of the soundtrack, they were credited as "The Goblins.")

In 1976, they collaborated with Willy Brezza to compose the score to *Perché si uccidono* (the film was released in the US as "Percy is Killed", but this was a mis-translation and there is no character even called Percy in the film). For the only time, Goblin used the name **Il Reale Impero Britannico** due to the controversial subject matter of the movie (drug abuse and addiction). [Willy Brezza](/w/index.php?title=Willy_Brezza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Willy Brezza (page does not exist)") wrote the original score, and the remaining tracks were written by the band together with [Fabio Frizzi](/wiki/Fabio_Frizzi "Fabio Frizzi"). The following year they scored the Italian crime film *[The Heroin Busters](/wiki/The_Heroin_Busters "The Heroin Busters")* (*La via della droga*), directed by [Enzo G. Castellari](/wiki/Enzo_G._Castellari "Enzo G. Castellari"), and starring [Fabio Testi](/wiki/Fabio_Testi "Fabio Testi") and [David Hemmings](/wiki/David_Hemmings "David Hemmings").

### Fragmentation, 1978–1985

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fragmentation, 1978–1985")]

Between 1978 and 1979, the band's core musicians recruited many new members consecutively.[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Fabio Pignatelli, Agostino Marangolo, his brother [Antonio Marangolo](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Marangolo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antonio Marangolo (page does not exist)") (a saxophonist who contributed to several albums) and nephew [Carlo Pennisi](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Pennisi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carlo Pennisi (page does not exist)") (a session man who often played in place of Massimo Morante when he was absent) all worked together from 1980 to 1982 in the band, Flea on the Honey, which managed to record several LPs. Pignatelli took part in all the recordings, with Agostino Marangolo ranking lead for number of performances. The remaining members continued to work on further scores, and there was a partial reunification for Argento's *Tenebrae* (1982) (although each member of the band was credited separately, not as Goblin). Over time, it was three of the "founding fathers" (Pignatelli, Simonetti and Morante), plus Marongolo, who became synonymous with the name Goblin.

### Reunion for *Sleepless* score, 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reunion for Sleepless score, 2000")]

After 22 years of hiatus, in 2000, the group reformed to score the Dario Argento thriller *Non ho sonno* (*Sleepless*). The score was a great success and showed the group could still make great music, much to their fans' delight. The group were scheduled to perform in [Tarrytown, New York](/wiki/Tarrytown,_New_York "Tarrytown, New York") for the infamous Cult-Con, but failed to appear. Simonetti did however appear at the show and informed fans that old wounds resurfaced during their brief reunion. With his horror theme tribute band, Daemonia (formed by Titta Tani, Bruno Previtali, Federico Amorosi, and Simonetti himself), he performed a nine-song set from the films of Dario Argento, and Goblin later officially disbanded.

### Back To The Goblin, 2005–2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Back To The Goblin, 2005–2009")]

#### *BackToTheGoblin2005*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: BackToTheGoblin2005")]

Nevertheless, in 2005, with the release of Giovanni Aloisio's official Goblin biography and the opening of their official website, Morante and Pignatelli reformed the group once again and with Marangolo and Guarini recorded the album *BackToTheGoblin2005* under the independent label BackToTheFudda. The album was available only through the official site[[1]](#cite_note-1) and was not distributed in regular stores. It is also available on iTunes.

#### 2009 reunion tour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2009 reunion tour")]

In 2009, Goblin, now **Back To The Goblin**, made their first live concert appearance in 32 years. Keyboardist [Aidan Zammit](/wiki/Aidan_Zammit "Aidan Zammit") has joined the band for their live performances. Excluding Simonetti, this is basically the "classic" line-up, with Maurizio Guarini contributing keyboard work to most of their original albums, playing on *Roller*, *Chi?*, *Suspiria*, *Buio Omega, Patrick, Contamination, St. Helens, Notturno, Volo, BackToTheGoblin* and more. The five members (Morante, Guarini, Pignatelli, Marangolo, and Aidan Zammit) performed a few concerts around Europe in 2009:

* [Donaufestival](/wiki/Donaufestival "Donaufestival") in Krems, [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") on 23 April 2009
* [La villette sonique](/w/index.php?title=La_villette_sonique&action=edit&redlink=1 "La villette sonique (page does not exist)") in Paris - 29 May
* [Dancity Festival](/w/index.php?title=Dancity_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dancity Festival (page does not exist)") at the Auditorium San Domenico - Foligno - 25 June
* [Supersonic Festival](/wiki/Supersonic_Festival_(Birmingham) "Supersonic Festival (Birmingham)") in Birmingham, UK - 26 July
* [Scala](/wiki/Scala_(club) "Scala (club)"), London - 27 July

In December 2009, Back to the Goblin once again announced their dissolution due to internal conflict. The remaining scheduled concerts into 2010 were then canceled.

### New Goblin/Goblin Rebirth and the Goblin Keys, 2010–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: New Goblin/Goblin Rebirth and the Goblin Keys, 2010–present")]

**New Goblin** were scheduled to perform at [Unsound Festival](/wiki/Unsound_Festival "Unsound Festival") [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") on 23 October 2010, consisting of Claudio Simonetti in the line-up, as well as Morante, Guarini, [Bruno Previtali](/w/index.php?title=Bruno_Previtali&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bruno Previtali (page does not exist)") and [Titta Tani](/w/index.php?title=Titta_Tani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Titta Tani (page does not exist)").[[2]](#cite_note-Unsound-2) Essentially, it was three members of the classic lineup and two members of Simonetti's other band, Daemonia (Previtali and Tani being the bassist and drummer, respectively, of that band).

Yet another incarnation of the band, formed in December 2010 and called **Goblin Rebirth**, includes former [Goblin](/wiki/Goblin "Goblin") members Fabio Pignatelli and Agostino Marangolo plus [Giacomo Anselmi](/w/index.php?title=Giacomo_Anselmi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giacomo Anselmi (page does not exist)") on guitars, Aidan Zammit and [Danilo Cherni](/w/index.php?title=Danilo_Cherni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Danilo Cherni (page does not exist)") on keyboards.

In 2011, the monumental 450-page book, *[Goblin sette note in rosso](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_sette_note_in_rosso&action=edit&redlink=1 "Goblin sette note in rosso (page does not exist)")* (*Goblin seven notes in red*) was released, written by Fabio Capuzzo. It traces the full history of Goblin from 1973 to 2011 with a detailed analysis of all the albums and movies with music performed by Goblin (including all the works as [session musicians](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician")). It also details full biographies, exclusive interviews and contains discographies and filmographies of the Italian composers who created scores for police, *[giallo](/wiki/Giallo "Giallo")* and horror movies, and information about all the Italian rock bands with a role in movie soundtracks.

In 2011/2012, New Goblin (Morante, Guarini, Simonetti, Previtali and Tani) had activity around the world, including Japan (twice), Australia (twice) and New Zealand.

In April 2012, Claudio Simonetti and now-[Greater Toronto](/wiki/Greater_Toronto "Greater Toronto") based [Maurizio Guarini](/wiki/Maurizio_Guarini "Maurizio Guarini") performed with drummer [Bob Scott](/w/index.php?title=Bob_Scott_(drummer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bob Scott (drummer) (page does not exist)") and guitarist [Chris Gartner](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Gartner_(rock)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chris Gartner (rock) (page does not exist)"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) in [Shock Stock](/w/index.php?title=Shock_Stock&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shock Stock (page does not exist)") 2012 on 14 April 2012 as **The Goblin Keys**.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
On 24 June 2013, the New Goblin line-up announced their first-ever North American tour and under their original banner, Goblin, played 17 dates in October 2013, with [Secret Chiefs 3](/wiki/Secret_Chiefs_3 "Secret Chiefs 3") as the opening act.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Tour and Simonetti's Goblin, 2013–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tour and Simonetti's Goblin, 2013–present")]

Simonetti has since formed another incarnation of the band called **Claudio Simonetti's Goblin**, which features him and the other members of his horror film theme cover group Daemonia.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In February 2014 they began touring and playing complete scores live during screenings of the films *Deep Red*, *Suspiria* and *Dawn of the Dead*. In April 2014, the band was slated to tour, performing the score to *Dawn of the Dead* live throughout North America, but in July the tour was canceled. In June 2014 Claudio Simonetti's Goblin released an album titled *[The Murder Collection](/w/index.php?title=The_Murder_Collection&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Murder Collection (page does not exist)")*, consisting of new versions of some of Goblin and Simonetti's most well-known compositions.

### 4/5 of original Goblin members reunion, live and studio activity, 2013–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 4/5 of original Goblin members reunion, live and studio activity, 2013–present")]

Goblin returned for a second US tour leg in December 2013 with Simonetti, Bruno Previtali and Titta Tani being replaced by original members Fabio Pignatelli and Agostino Marangolo and additional keyboard player Aidan Zammit. The reunited band played 13 dates mostly in Midwest US.

In April and May 2014, Goblin returned in US for the third time. Aidan Zammit was replaced by Steve Moore of the band Zombi and Goblin played nine dates throughout the southern United States.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In October 2014, Massimo Morante, Maurizio Guarini, Fabio Pignatelli and Agostino Marangolo announced that, as Goblin, they released an album of new material titled *[Four of a Kind](/w/index.php?title=Four_of_a_Kind_(Goblin_album)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Four of a Kind (Goblin album) (page does not exist)")* and started a crowdfunding campaign through the website pledgemusic.com to help complete it.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In June 2015, Goblin Rebirth released their self-titled debut album and did various live appearances.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Also in 2015, Cherry Five reformed with original members Carlo Bordini and Tony Tartarini along with new members Ludovico Piccinini (guitars), Gianluca De Rossi (keyboards) and Pino Sallusti (bass) and released an album titled *Il Pozzo dei Giganti*.[[7]](#cite_note-7) On 3 April 2017. bassist Pino Sallusti died.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In early 2017, Goblin (Morante, Guarini, Pignatelli, Marangolo with Aidan Zammit) resumed live performance with three concerts in Europe: Sweden, Norway and Greece.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 26 October 2017 through 12 November, Goblin toured North America on their 'Sound of Fear' tour with 13 dates in US and 2 in Canada.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In October 2017, the US publishing house Ajna published Fabio Capuzzo's *Goblin Seven Notes in Red*, the full revised and updated English version of the Italian book *Goblin sette note in rosso*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In May 2018, Goblin appeared on the debut full-length Princess album with a new song called "God Save the Goblin" (Pignatelli/Wolf/Guarini/Morante/Marangolo) plus a couple of featuring (one by Fabio Pignatelli and another by Tony Tartarini).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Goblin appeared at the Psycho Las Vegas on 19 August 2018.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A Goblin album, *Fearless*, was released at the end of 2018.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Claudio Simonetti's Goblin actively toured Europe and North America from 2019 - 2022 with a break for COVID.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Massimo Morante died in June 2022, at the age of 69.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2023, Giacomo Anselmi former member of Goblin Rebirth joined Goblin on guitar, replacing Massimo.

In August 2024, due to internal conflict, both Agostino Marangolo and Fabio Pignatelli have left Goblin.
Original Goblin drummer Walter Martino and "Fabio Frizzi Live" bassist Roberto Fasciani have since replaced them both.
The band will now be known as **Goblin Legacy**.

Walter Martino died in [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") on 7 March 2026, at the age of 72.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Style and influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goblin_(band)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Style and influences")]

[AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic")'s review of Goblin's soundtrack for *[Deep Red](/wiki/Deep_Red "Deep Red")* describes the score as "an ambitious affair that blends [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [prog rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), and [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") into an effective and totally distinctive style"[[13]](#cite_note-allmusic-13) and the track "Deep Shadows" as "a frenetic slice of [King Crimson](/wiki/King_Crimson "King Crimson")-style [jazz-rock](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion")".[[13]](#cite_note-allmusic-13)

Influences cited by Goblin include [Yes](/wiki/Yes_(band) "Yes (band)"), [Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_(band) "Genesis (band)"), [King Crimson](/wiki/King_Crimson "King Crimson"), [Gentle Giant](/wiki/Gentle_Giant "Gentle Giant") and [Emerson, Lake & Palmer](/wiki/Emerson,_Lake_%26_Palmer "Emerson, Lake & Palmer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The band [Zombi](/wiki/Zombi_(band) "Zombi (band)") was influenced by Goblin.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The band [Opeth](/wiki/Opeth "Opeth") included an instrumental titled "Goblin" on their eleventh album, *[Pale Communion](/wiki/Pale_Communion "Pale Communion")*, in tribute to the band.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
